summary: McKenzie discovers that a new version of his software has broken critical features on the Mac platform, including saving, printing, and displaying. The bug resulted in at least one customer refund, which he finds particularly painful since it means losing someone who had already committed to purchasing. He reverts the Mac version and PAD file while working on a fix, crediting a colleague for help debugging.
key_points: Cross-platform Java development creates platform-specific bugs that are hard to catch before release
Losing an existing paying customer is far worse than failing to convert a prospect
Quick reversion to a known-good state is the pragmatic response to a broken release
money_quotes: Just when I think its safe to push out a new version I find that the Mac code has been severely compromised somehow and minor features like saving, printing, and displaying no longer work.
annoying people who pay money for software they haven't even downloaded yet, thats like kicking yourself firmly in the teeth
key_insight: Paying customers who encounter broken software represent a uniquely painful failure because you've already won their trust and money, then immediately betrayed both.
